common_name: Sieva Bean (Baby Lima Bean, Carolina Lima Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, warm soil in full sun; sieva beans (baby limas) are the small-seeded lima bean type traditional to the southeastern United States — faster-maturing, more heat-tolerant, and less prone to poor pod set in hot weather than large-seeded Fordhook-type lima beans, making them far superior for the Gulf Coast summer garden.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; provide support for pole types; pick pods when full and plump but still green. Phaseolus lunatus var. sieva (sieva bean or baby lima bean) is the small-seeded form of lima bean — traditionally called 'butter beans' across the South — that has been cultivated in the southeastern United States for centuries, adapted to the hot, humid conditions of the Gulf Coast and Deep South where the large-seeded Fordhook-type limas are far less reliable. Sieva beans are smaller (1/3 to 1/2 the seed size of Fordhook), have thinner seed coats, cook more quickly, and are more heat-tolerant at pollination — a critical advantage in Gulf Coast summers where high night temperatures (above 70°F) cause heavy-seeded limas to drop flowers and fail to set pods. Heritage varieties like 'Florida Speckled' (with beautiful purple-speckled green seeds), 'Henderson Bush,' and 'Christmas Butter Bean' are treasured in Southern heirloom seed collections. Fresh sieva beans (butter beans) are a beloved Southern side dish — simmered with fatback or butter until creamy-tender, they have a rich, buttery, starchy flavor distinct from Northern-style green limas. Also excellent dried and shelled for winter use.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Lima Bean Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Vegetable Gardening
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Lima Bean Production

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–14 weeks before desired transplant date (July–August in zone 9 for a fall transplant); plant out in September–October in moderately fertile, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moisture-retentive soil in full sun; Romanesco produces its characteristic spiraling, lime-green fractal heads (a naturally occurring example of a logarithmic spiral and Fibonacci sequence) and has a milder, nuttier flavor than either standard broccoli or cauliflower.

directions: Start seeds indoors 10–14 weeks before transplanting; plant in moderately fertile, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moisture-retentive soil in full sun; cover heads as they develop to maintain lime-green color if desired. Brassica oleracea var. botrytis 'Romanesco' (Romanesco or Roman broccoli) is a variety of Italian origin — known since the 16th century in Rome — that produces one of the most visually spectacular vegetables in existence: a tightly packed head of lime-green, spiraling conical florets arranged in a precise logarithmic spiral following the Fibonacci sequence, creating a fractal pattern of remarkable mathematical beauty. Each floret replicates the overall head shape at a smaller scale, in a perfect example of self-similar fractal geometry visible to the naked eye. The flavor is equally noteworthy: milder, sweeter, and nuttier than both standard broccoli and cauliflower, with a crisp-firm texture that holds up well when roasted or steamed. Romanesco requires similar growing conditions to cauliflower — it is the most temperature-sensitive of the three major brassica heading types, requiring consistent temperatures during head formation for proper development; heat causes premature bolting or loose, irregular heads. In zone 9, grow as a fall crop, planted September–October for December–February harvest. The stunning heads make excellent centerpiece dishes and are highly popular at farmers markets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Brassica Production
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Romanesco Cultural Information
Slow Food Foundation — Romanesco di Verona Ark of Taste

common_name: Purple Carrot (Cosmic Purple Carrot, Anthocyanin Carrot)

planting.note: Direct sow fall through early winter (September–November in zone 9) in deeply tilled, loose, stone-free, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Cosmic Purple carrot has deep purple-skinned roots with orange and yellow interior flesh — the purple color from anthocyanin pigments intensifies in cool weather and makes this one of the most nutritionally rich carrot varieties available to home growers.

directions: Direct sow seeds thinly (1/4 inch deep) in deeply tilled, loose, stone-free, lean soil in full sun; thin to 2–3 inches; harvest at 6–8 inches for best color and flavor. Daucus carota 'Cosmic Purple' (purple carrot or anthocyanin carrot) is an open-pollinated purple-rooted carrot variety developed by the USDA Agricultural Research Service — one of the most richly pigmented and nutritionally dense carrot varieties available to home growers. The root has deep purple to nearly black outer skin from very high anthocyanin concentrations (the same antioxidant pigments found in blueberries and red cabbage) with orange interior flesh, creating a dramatic color contrast when sliced. The flavor is significantly different from standard orange carrots: 'Cosmic Purple' is notably sweeter, with a mild, distinctive flavor complexity from both sugars and secondary compounds. Anthocyanins in the skin are heat-labile and can fade during cooking — roasting at high heat preserves them better than boiling or steaming. Eating purple carrots raw in salads or quick-pickling with vinegar (which intensifies the purple to fuchsia-red) are excellent ways to enjoy both flavor and color. In zone 9, purple carrots are planted in fall for winter harvest when cool temperatures concentrate sugars; spring-planted carrots in warm areas tend to be less sweet and may bolt before fully sizing. A beautiful addition to diverse carrot sowings alongside orange, yellow, and white types.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Purple Carrot Breeding
University of Wisconsin Extension — Specialty Carrots

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moisture-retentive soil in full sun to part shade; shiso (Japanese basil) is an indispensable herb in Japanese and Korean cuisine — the fringed, aromatic leaves come in green (ao-shiso) and red/purple (aka-shiso) forms, each with a distinctive flavor combining basil, mint, anise, and cinnamon; red shiso is also used to color umeboshi (pickled plums) and to make perilla vinegar.

directions: Direct sow seeds on the soil surface (needs light to germinate) in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; shiso self-seeds prolifically once established. Perilla frutescens var. crispa (shiso or Japanese basil) is an East Asian annual herb — one of the most important culinary herbs in Japanese cuisine — with large, ruffled, deeply crenate (toothy-edged), intensely aromatic leaves in green (ao-shiso) or red-purple (aka-shiso) forms, each with a distinctive, complex flavor combining basil, mint, anise, cinnamon, and fresh herbal notes unlike any other culinary herb. In Japanese cooking, green shiso leaves are used fresh and whole to wrap sashimi and sushi (providing a fresh, aromatic counterpoint to raw fish), cut into chiffonade as a garnish for cold noodles and soups, and fried whole in tempura batter. Red/purple shiso leaves are used to color and flavor umeboshi (salt-pickled sour plums) a bright magenta-pink, to make perilla vinegar, and in Korean cuisine to make kkaennip kimchi (perilla leaf kimchi). The seeds are also edible and are used in Japanese cooking as a condiment. Shiso tolerates partial shade better than most herbs and thrives in the heat and humidity of Southern summers; it self-seeds abundantly in warm climates, effectively naturalizing in the garden once established. The plant is nutritionally rich in vitamins A and C, rosmarinic acid, and luteolin.
sources: Japan Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries — Shiso
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Herbs

planting.note: Plant small whole tubers or cut tuber pieces in spring in well-drained, loose, humus-rich, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; cush cush is the only domesticated yam native to the Americas — a South American and Caribbean vine yam producing high-quality, purple-fleshed or white-fleshed tubers with excellent flavor and a smooth, non-mucilaginous texture; it was an important food crop of Caribbean indigenous peoples before European contact.

directions: Plant small tubers in loose, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; provide trellis for the climbing vine. Dioscorea trifida (cush cush yam or mapuey) is the only major cultivated yam native to the Americas — all other important yams (D. alata, D. cayenensis, D. rotundata) originate from Asia or Africa. Native to the Caribbean, Central America, and the Amazon Basin, where it was cultivated as an important food crop by indigenous peoples before European contact, cush cush yam produces distinctive tubers — often three-lobed (hence 'trifida' and the name 'cush cush') — with high-quality flesh ranging from white to cream to purple-violet, depending on variety. The flesh is notably smooth and non-mucilaginous (unlike many Asian yams), with a mildly sweet, earthy, chestnut-like flavor. Cush cush yam is considered one of the finest-flavored yams in terms of culinary quality: the texture cooks to a smooth, dry, mealy consistency similar to the best potatoes. Purple-fleshed varieties have high anthocyanin content and a spectacular color. The vine is ornamental — the deeply three-lobed, arrowhead-shaped leaves are distinctive and attractive. One of the most overlooked Native American food crops, now rare even in its home region.
sources: CIAT — Andean Root and Tuber Crops
University of the West Indies — Caribbean Food Plants
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Dioscorea trifida

common_name: Sweet Calabash (Sweet Granadilla, Hard-Shell Passion Fruit)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or seedlings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; sweet calabash is a Caribbean and northern South American passionflower that produces round, hard-shelled, brownish-yellow fruits with intensely sweet, aromatic, orange-yellow juice — considered by many Caribbean islanders to be the best-flavored of all wild passionfruits, far sweeter than common purple passionfruit.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; provide very strong trellis or arbor support. Passiflora maliformis (sweet calabash or sweet granadilla) is a passionflower native to the Caribbean islands and northern South America that produces one of the sweetest and most intensely aromatic of all passionfruits. The fruits are rounded to slightly apple-shaped, roughly 2–3 inches across, with a hard, woody, yellow-brown to dark-brown shell when ripe (much harder than purple or yellow passionfruit shells), protecting an extremely sweet, aromatic, orange-yellow pulp with a flavor that combines passion fruit, apricot, and guava with a honeyed sweetness rarely found in cultivated passionfruits. The hard shell makes it less susceptible to insect damage than thin-shelled species, and the fruits can be stored for weeks at room temperature. To eat, crack the hard shell and scoop out the aromatic pulp with seeds; the juice is excellent mixed with water as a tropical drink. Sweet calabash is prized in Caribbean home gardens and is occasionally grown in tropical botanical gardens worldwide; it remains very little known in commercial cultivation despite its outstanding fruit quality. The attractive, divided leaves and intricate blue-and-white flowers are typical of the genus. In zone 10–12, the vine is vigorous and productive in full sun.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Passionfruits
Purdue University Center for New Crops — Passiflora maliformis
